writing_task: Hey, I want to put together a quick reference for the token refresh flow specifically — we keep getting questions about it in onboarding and I think a standalone doc would help. It should cover when tokens expire, how to trigger a refresh, and what to do when the refresh itself fails. Same general audience as the auth guide — competent engineers, just new to this system.

drafts.rubric:
Token Refresh Flow: Quick Reference

Before you read the steps below, note two things up front. First, your access token has a 15-minute TTL. This is intentionally short — we considered 60-minute tokens and even sliding-window sessions, but both expand the blast radius when a token is compromised. Fifteen minutes bounds the exposure window without requiring re-authentication on every call, because the refresh token (7-day TTL, rotating on each use) handles continuity silently. Second, the refresh endpoint requires your client to be registered in the service registry before any of this works. If you skip that step, every refresh attempt returns 401 with no useful error detail, and that's the number one question we get from new engineers.

When your access token expires, the API returns 401 with error code TOKEN_EXPIRED in the response body. That's your signal to call POST /auth/token/refresh with your current refresh token in the Authorization header as Bearer {refresh_token}. Do not retry the original request first — the retry will also fail, and under high load that wastes quota.

A successful refresh returns a new access_token and a new refresh_token. Replace both immediately. The old refresh token is invalidated on use, so if you cache the old one and use it again (a common mistake when refresh logic sits in a shared cache layer with stale writes), you'll get REFRESH_TOKEN_REUSE and be locked out until a full re-auth.

When the refresh itself fails, here's what each error means and what to do:

"My refresh call is returning 401 / REFRESH_TOKEN_EXPIRED" — the 7-day window passed, or the token was already rotated by another instance of your service. The only path forward is a full re-auth via POST /auth/token. Make sure your refresh logic is not racing across multiple service replicas; use a distributed lock or route refresh calls through a single replica.

"I'm getting 400 / INVALID_CLIENT even though my credentials look right" — your client_id or client_secret doesn't match what's in the registry. Verify in the service registry UI at registry.internal/clients. Mismatches here are almost always a config drift between environments.

"The refresh succeeds but I immediately get TOKEN_EXPIRED on the next call" — clock skew. The token's iat and exp are server-stamped. If your host clock is more than 30 seconds off, tokens appear expired on arrival. Check NTP sync. We chose server-side timestamping over client-side to eliminate a whole class of clock-manipulation attacks, at the cost of this skew sensitivity.

"Everything works locally but fails in staging" — staging uses a separate token issuer at auth-staging.internal, not auth.internal. This is intentional: staging tokens must not be usable in production. Update your AUTH_BASE_URL environment variable.

For local development, set AUTH_BASE_URL=http://localhost:9000 and run the auth emulator (docker run --rm -p 9000:9000 internal/auth-emulator:latest). The emulator issues real-format tokens with a configurable TTL via the DEV_TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS env var, so you can test expiry behavior without waiting 15 minutes.

Sample refresh call in Go:

req, _ := http.NewRequest("POST", baseURL+"/auth/token/refresh", nil)
req.Header.Set("Authorization", "Bearer "+refreshToken)
resp, err := client.Do(req)
// On success: parse resp body for access_token and refresh_token, store both.
// On 401: trigger full re-auth. Do not retry refresh.

Error reference:

TOKEN_EXPIRED (401): access token is past TTL — call refresh.
REFRESH_TOKEN_EXPIRED (401): refresh token past 7-day TTL — full re-auth required.
REFRESH_TOKEN_REUSE (401): refresh token already rotated — likely a caching bug or race; full re-auth required.
INVALID_CLIENT (400): client credentials mismatch — check registry.
RATE_LIMITED (429): too many refresh attempts — back off exponentially, minimum 1s base delay.

Related resources: auth-service proto definitions at go/auth-proto, full auth architecture doc at go/auth-guide, service registry at registry.internal/clients, runbook for mass token revocation events at go/auth-runbook, #auth-help Slack channel for anything not covered here.
drafts.generic:
Access tokens expire after 60 minutes. When a request returns a 401 status, your client should automatically attempt a refresh before retrying. To refresh, send a POST request to /auth/refresh with your refresh token in the request body. A successful response returns a new access token and a new refresh token — replace both in your stored credentials immediately, as the old refresh token is invalidated.

If the refresh request itself returns a 401 or 403, the refresh token has expired or been revoked. At that point, the session cannot be recovered automatically. Redirect the user through the full authentication flow to obtain new credentials.
drafts.preference:
Token Refresh Flow

Tokens issued by this system expire after 60 minutes. The expiration timestamp is included in the token payload as `exp` (Unix time), so you can check it directly rather than waiting for a 401.

Triggering a Refresh

When a token is near expiry, POST to `/auth/refresh` with your refresh token:

```
POST /auth/refresh
{ "refresh_token": "eyJ..." }
```

A successful response returns a new access token and a new refresh token. Replace both — refresh tokens are single-use here, which differs from some systems you may have used.

When the Refresh Fails

A failed refresh returns 401 with `"code": "refresh_expired"`. At that point the session is unrecoverable; redirect the user to re-authenticate. Do not retry — the refresh token is invalidated on failure.

```
if (response.status === 401 && body.code === "refresh_expired") {
  redirectToLogin();
}
```

writing_task: I need to document the logout flow next — specifically how token revocation works when a user explicitly logs out versus when a token just expires naturally. Can you write up a section that covers the revocation endpoint, what happens to any active refresh tokens, and how downstream services should handle requests that come in with a revoked token? Same audience as before: competent engineers who are new to this system.

drafts.rubric:
Logout and Token Revocation

Before you implement anything in this section, confirm that your service is registered in the token registry and has read access to the revocation store. Services that skip this check will silently accept revoked tokens because the validation path won't know where to look. If you're unsure whether your service is registered, check the service registry at go/token-registry or ask in #auth-platform.

Why explicit logout and natural expiry are handled differently

When a token simply expires, nothing needs to be written anywhere. The expiry claim in the token is self-contained, so any service can reject it locally without a network call. We considered using the same path for explicit logout — just waiting for tokens to expire — but the blast radius was unacceptable. A user who logs out after losing a device could have up to 24 hours of valid tokens still circulating. Forcing logout to rely on expiry would mean we couldn't actually terminate sessions.

Explicit logout, by contrast, requires active revocation: we write a record to the revocation store so services can catch tokens that are technically unexpired but should no longer be honored.

How to call the revocation endpoint

> Warning: The revocation endpoint is synchronous only for the primary access token. Refresh token revocation propagates asynchronously with up to a 60-second lag. Do not assume a successful revocation response means all tokens are immediately dead everywhere.

Call POST /auth/v2/revoke with the access token in the Authorization header and the refresh token in the request body. The endpoint returns 200 on success. It invalidates the access token immediately, writes to the revocation store, and enqueues an async job to walk and invalidate the entire refresh token family.

// Revoke an access token and its associated refresh token family
curl -X POST https://auth.internal/auth/v2/revoke \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <access_token>" \
  -d '{"refresh_token": "<refresh_token>"}' \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json"

We chose to revoke the entire refresh token family (all tokens derived from the original grant) rather than just the presented refresh token. The alternative — revoking only the leaf token — would leave sibling tokens alive if the client had silently rotated. The added complexity of family tracking is justified by the guarantee that logout is actually complete.

What your service does when it receives a revoked token

> Warning: Do not rely solely on local expiry checks for authorization decisions. A token that passes local validation may still be revoked. Always check the revocation store for user-facing write operations.

For read operations, local expiry validation is acceptable. For writes and privileged actions, your service must call the revocation check endpoint before proceeding.

// Check whether a token has been explicitly revoked before processing a write
GET https://auth.internal/auth/v2/revocation-status?jti=<token_jti>

The jti claim is in the token payload. A 200 response with {"revoked": false} means the token is clean. Treat any other response as revoked and return 401 to the caller.

Expected error states

401 from /auth/v2/revoke — the access token was already expired or malformed. Log and treat the session as terminated; nothing further is needed.

404 from /auth/v2/revocation-status — the jti is not in the store, which means the token predates the revocation store migration (cutover was 2023-11-01). Treat tokens with no record and an issue date before that cutover as valid; reject anything newer with no record.

503 from /auth/v2/revocation-status — the revocation store is unavailable. Your service should fall back to local expiry validation and log the degraded check. Do not hard-fail user requests on store outages; the risk of availability impact outweighed the security benefit of strict enforcement during outages.

Testing this locally

Run the auth stack locally with docker compose up -f docker/auth.yml. The local environment stubs the async refresh token family walk synchronously so you can test full revocation in a single request without waiting. See go/auth-local-dev for the full setup guide and go/auth-runbooks for operational playbooks. Questions go to #auth-platform.
drafts.generic:
When a user explicitly logs out, your client should call the revocation endpoint (POST /auth/revoke), passing the current access token and any associated refresh tokens. The server immediately invalidates both, adding them to a blocklist checked on every subsequent request. Natural token expiration, by contrast, requires no active revocation — tokens simply stop being accepted once their exp claim passes.

The critical difference is refresh token handling. On explicit logout, all refresh tokens tied to that session are purged, preventing silent re-authentication. On natural expiry, refresh tokens may remain valid until their own expiration.

Downstream services authenticate requests by validating the token signature, checking the exp claim, and consulting the blocklist. If a token appears on the blocklist, services should return a 401 and must not fall back to cached authorization decisions. Clients receiving a 401 in this context should redirect users to the login flow rather than attempting to refresh, since the refresh token is equally invalid.
drafts.preference:
Token Revocation and Logout

Explicit logout vs. token expiry

When a token expires naturally, nothing active happens — the token simply becomes invalid when the current time passes the exp claim. No revocation record is written, and no downstream notification is sent. Explicit logout is different: the client calls the revocation endpoint, which immediately marks the token as invalid regardless of its expiry time.

Calling the revocation endpoint

Send a POST to /auth/revoke with the access token or refresh token in the request body:

POST /auth/revoke
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiJ9...
token_type_hint=access_token

The token_type_hint field is optional but speeds up lookup. The endpoint accepts both access tokens and refresh tokens. On success it returns 200 with an empty body — it returns 200 even if the token was already invalid or unrecognized, per RFC 7009, so do not treat a 200 as confirmation the token was previously active.

What happens to refresh tokens

If you revoke an access token, the associated refresh token is also immediately revoked. The reverse is also true. This system treats the access/refresh pair as a single session unit, which differs from implementations where each token is revoked independently. If the user has multiple active sessions — say, a mobile client and a browser session — revoking one session's tokens does not affect the others. Each session holds a distinct token pair.

How downstream services should handle revoked tokens

Downstream services validate tokens by checking the signature and expiry claim. That catches expired tokens but not revoked ones, because revocation state lives in the token store, not in the token itself. To catch revoked tokens, services must check the token store on each request.

The token store exposes a lightweight introspection endpoint:

POST /auth/introspect
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiJ9...

A valid, unrevoked token returns:

{
  "active": true,
  "sub": "user-8821",
  "exp": 1714000000,
  "scope": "read write"
}

A revoked or expired token returns:

{
  "active": false
}

When active is false, the downstream service should return 401 to the caller. Do not attempt to refresh the token on the service side — that is the client's responsibility. The expected client behavior is to catch the 401, attempt to obtain a new access token using the refresh token, and if that also fails (because the refresh token is revoked), redirect the user to login.

Caching introspection results

Calling introspection on every request adds latency. Services may cache a positive introspection result, but the cache TTL must not exceed the token's remaining lifetime, and it must be invalidated immediately on any 401 response from a peer service or from the introspection endpoint itself. Do not cache negative results — an active: false response should always be treated as definitive and acted on immediately.

writing_task: Hey, I want to document the token refresh flow next — specifically how services should handle the case where a token is about to expire and needs to be refreshed without interrupting an in-flight request. Can you draft that section? Same audience as before: competent engineers who are new to this specific system.

drafts.generic: When a token is nearing expiration, the service should not wait for it to fully expire before acting. Instead, each service checks the token's expiration timestamp before making an outbound request. If the token will expire within a configurable threshold — typically sixty seconds — the service triggers a background refresh while still using the existing token to complete the in-flight request. The refresh runs asynchronously, acquiring a new token from the auth service and replacing the stored credential. Subsequent requests then use the refreshed token. If the refresh fails, the service retries with exponential backoff before surfacing an error to the caller.

writing_task: Alright, the token refresh section is in good shape. Next I need to document the token revocation flow — specifically how the system handles invalidating tokens across multiple service instances when a user logs out or changes their password. Can you draft that section, keeping the same structure we've been using: warning block first, then the architectural decision with what we rejected, then a minimal code example?

drafts.rubric:
> Before implementing token revocation, confirm that your service is registered with the Token Invalidation Bus (TIB). Revocation calls made from unregistered services silently succeed on the caller's side but are not propagated — other instances will continue accepting the token until it expires naturally. Register via the service registry at go/tib-register before proceeding.

---

Why revocation is harder than it looks

A JWT is stateless by design: any instance that holds the signing key can validate it without a network call. That property becomes a liability at logout. When a user logs out or changes their password, you need every running instance — potentially dozens — to stop accepting that token immediately, not when it expires.

The naive approach is to check a central blocklist on every request. We rejected this because it reintroduces the latency and availability coupling that JWTs were chosen to avoid: if the blocklist store degrades, all token validation degrades with it.

We also considered shortening token lifetimes to reduce the exposure window. A 60-second TTL would bound the worst case, but it shifts the burden to the refresh flow and increases load on the auth service by roughly one order of magnitude under observed traffic patterns.

The chosen approach publishes a revocation event to TIB, which fans the event out to all registered service instances over a persistent gRPC stream. Each instance maintains an in-process denied-token set, keyed by `jti`, with a TTL matching the token's original expiry. This bounds memory growth while guaranteeing that no instance accepts a revoked token beyond the propagation window, which is under 500ms at the 99th percentile.

How to call revocation at logout

Call `AuthService.RevokeToken` with the user's current access token and, if present, their refresh token. Both must be passed in the same call — a separate revocation call for each would create a race window where the refresh token could be used to mint a new access token after the access token is already blocked.

```go
// Revoke both tokens atomically to prevent refresh-token replay after logout
resp, err := authClient.RevokeToken(ctx, &authpb.RevokeTokenRequest{
    AccessToken:  currentAccessToken,
    RefreshToken: currentRefreshToken, // omit only if no refresh token was issued
})
if err != nil {
    // See error table below — do not treat all errors as retryable
    return fmt.Errorf("revocation failed: %w", err)
}
if !resp.Acknowledged {
    // TIB accepted the call but propagation could not be confirmed; see RUNBOOK-0041
    log.Warn("revocation unacknowledged", "jti", resp.TokenId)
}
```

The `Acknowledged` field is false when TIB receives the event but cannot confirm fan-out to all instances — typically because one or more instances lost their stream connection in the preceding 10 seconds. In this state the token is queued for redelivery, but you should alert on sustained `Acknowledged: false` rates above 1% because it indicates stream instability.

What to do when revocation calls go wrong

| gRPC status | Meaning | Action |
|---|---|---|
| `OK` with `Acknowledged: false` | Event accepted, fan-out unconfirmed | Log and monitor; do not retry immediately |
| `INVALID_ARGUMENT` | Token is malformed or already expired | No action needed; expired tokens are inert |
| `NOT_FOUND` | `jti` is unknown to TIB | Token was never registered (pre-TIB issuance); treat as revoked for your service, file a bug |
| `UNAUTHENTICATED` | Caller service is not TIB-registered | Complete registration at go/tib-register before retrying |
| `UNAVAILABLE` | TIB is unreachable | Retry with exponential backoff, cap at 3 attempts; escalate to #auth-oncall if unresolved |

Local development

In local and staging environments, TIB runs as a sidecar in the auth-dev Docker Compose stack (`docker compose up tib`). The sidecar does not fan out to other instances — it acknowledges all calls immediately and logs events to stdout. This is intentional: local dev does not simulate multi-instance propagation. To test propagation behavior, use the shared staging cluster and consult the setup guide at go/tib-local-dev.

Related resources: proto definition at go/auth-proto, revocation runbook at RUNBOOK-0041, oncall at #auth-oncall.
drafts.generic:
> Warning: Token revocation is not instantaneous. A revoked token may remain valid on other service instances until the allowlist check propagates or the token's natural expiry elapses. Never rely on revocation alone for hard security boundaries.

When a user logs out or changes their password, the system writes the token's jti claim to a shared Redis revocation store with a TTL matching the token's remaining lifetime. Every service instance checks this store on each authenticated request. We rejected a broadcast invalidation approach using pub/sub messaging because it introduced race conditions during high-traffic spikes and provided no durability guarantee if a subscriber missed the event.

```python
def revoke_token(jti: str, ttl_seconds: int):
    redis_client.setex(f"revoked:{jti}", ttl_seconds, "1")

def is_revoked(jti: str) -> bool:
    return redis_client.exists(f"revoked:{jti}") == 1
```
drafts.preference:
Token Revocation

> Warning: Token revocation is not instantaneous across all service instances. There is a propagation window, configurable but defaulting to 30 seconds, during which a revoked token may still be accepted by instances that have not yet refreshed their local cache.

When a user logs out or changes their password, the auth service writes the token's jti claim to a shared Redis revocation list with a TTL matching the token's remaining lifetime. We rejected a broadcast invalidation approach — pushing revocation events to every instance via a message queue — because it introduced tight coupling and failure modes during queue outages. The pull model accepts a bounded staleness window in exchange for resilience.

```python
def revoke_token(jti: str, ttl_seconds: int) -> None:
    redis_client.setex(f"revoked:{jti}", ttl_seconds, "1")

def is_revoked(jti: str) -> bool:
    return redis_client.exists(f"revoked:{jti}") == 1
```

drafts.rubric:
# Token Introspection Endpoint

## Before You Write Any Validation Code

> **Prerequisite:** Your service must be registered with the auth platform before making introspection calls. Registration provides you a `client_id` and `client_secret` used to authenticate the introspection request itself. Without these, every call returns `401 Unauthorized` — not a malformed token response, but a flat rejection of your service's identity. Complete registration at `auth-platform.internal/register` before proceeding.

> **Warning — clock skew:** If you're considering local JWT validation, note that the platform's token-signing nodes operate on NTP-synced clocks, but your service may not. A skew of more than 30 seconds causes false `exp` failures in local validation. Introspection sidesteps this entirely because expiry evaluation happens server-side.

---

## Deciding Whether to Call Introspection or Decode Locally

You have two paths for validating an inbound token: decode the JWT locally using the platform's published public keys, or call the introspection endpoint and let the auth server evaluate the token authoritatively.

**Local decoding** is faster — it eliminates a network hop and adds roughly 0–2ms of crypto overhead versus 15–80ms round-trip for introspection under normal load. Choose local decoding when:

- The token is being validated on a hot path (e.g., every inbound gRPC frame)
- The only signal you need is "is this token structurally valid and unexpired?"
- Revocation is not a concern for your threat model (see below)

**Introspection is required when:**

- The user or service account may have had their token revoked mid-session (e.g., account suspension, credential rotation, forced logout)
- You need claims that aren't embedded in the JWT — specifically `scope` grants added after token issuance, or dynamic entitlements managed outside the token payload
- You are a high-privilege service where acting on a revoked token has a large blast radius (e.g., billing, user data export, admin operations)

The rejected alternative here — validating only locally for all cases — was ruled out because revoked tokens remain cryptographically valid until expiry. A token with a 24-hour TTL issued to a compromised credential would pass local validation for up to 24 hours after revocation. The blast radius on privileged services made this unacceptable.

A second rejected alternative — requiring introspection for every request on every service — was ruled out on performance grounds. At p99, introspection adds 120ms under load. Applying that unconditionally to high-frequency internal services would materially degrade latency budgets. The tiered approach — introspect at privilege boundaries, decode locally elsewhere — keeps the overhead targeted.

---

## Setting Up Your Service to Call the Endpoint

Register your service at `https://auth-platform.internal/register` and retain the `client_id` and `client_secret` from the response. These credentials authenticate your service to the introspection endpoint via HTTP Basic Auth. Store them in your secrets manager, not in environment variables committed to source control.

The introspection endpoint is:

```
POST https://auth-platform.internal/oauth2/introspect
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Authorization: Basic <base64(client_id:client_secret)>

token=<token_to_validate>
```

Pass the raw token string — do not decode or strip the signature before sending.

---

## Making the Introspection Call

```python
# Send a token to the introspection endpoint and return the parsed response body.
import base64
import httpx

def introspect_token(token: str, client_id: str, client_secret: str) -> dict:
    credentials = base64.b64encode(f"{client_id}:{client_secret}".encode()).decode()
    response = httpx.post(
        "https://auth-platform.internal/oauth2/introspect",
        headers={
            "Authorization": f"Basic {credentials}",
            "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
        },
        data={"token": token},
        timeout=5.0,  # 5s chosen to exceed p99.9 server latency (measured at 3.2s) while failing fast on hung connections
    )
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response.json()
```

The 5-second timeout reflects the measured p99.9 server response time of 3.2 seconds. A lower value (e.g., 2 seconds) would produce false timeout failures during transient auth-platform load spikes; a higher value (e.g., 10 seconds) would allow a degraded auth platform to hold open connections long enough to exhaust your service's connection pool.

---

## Reading the Response Fields

A successful call returns a JSON object conforming to RFC 7662. The fields your service should act on:

**`active` (boolean, always present)**
The only field guaranteed present. `true` means the token is valid, unexpired, and not revoked as of the time of this call. `false` means any of: revoked, expired, malformed, or issued by an untrusted issuer. When `active` is `false`, no other fields are reliable — the auth platform deliberately omits them to avoid leaking information about invalid tokens.

**`sub` (string)**
The subject of the token — typically a user UUID or service account identifier. This is the canonical identifier you should use for downstream authorization checks. Do not use the `preferred_username` or `email` fields for identity decisions; those can change.

**`scope` (space-delimited string)**
The granted scopes as evaluated at the time of the call. This may differ from the `scope` claim embedded in the JWT if scope grants were modified post-issuance. For services that make authorization decisions based on scope, always use the introspection response value, not the JWT claim.

**`exp` (integer, Unix epoch)**
Expiry timestamp. Present when `active` is `true`. You don't need to evaluate this yourself — the `active` field already accounts for it — but it's useful for logging and for decisions about whether to cache the result (covered below).

**`iat` (integer, Unix epoch)**
Issuance timestamp. Useful for detecting unusually old tokens being replayed.

**`client_id` (string)**
The OAuth client that issued this token. Use this to enforce service-to-service trust boundaries: if your service should only accept tokens issued by a specific upstream, assert this field.

**`token_type` (string)**
Either `bearer` or `dpop`. If your service requires DPoP-bound tokens (i.e., you've opted into sender-constrained tokens to prevent relay attacks), reject any response where this is `bearer`.

---

## Caching Introspection Results to Reduce Latency

Calling introspection on every request defeats the performance rationale for using it selectively. Cache the result, keyed by the full token string (or its SHA-256 hash, to avoid storing raw tokens in your cache layer).

Set cache TTL to the minimum of:
- 60 seconds (hard cap regardless of token expiry)
- `exp - now - 30` seconds (expire the cache entry 30 seconds before the token expires)

The 60-second cap is not arbitrary: the auth platform propagates revocations to all signing nodes within 45 seconds under normal conditions. Caching beyond 60 seconds means your service may act on a revoked token for up to 15 seconds past the propagation window — a gap your security team has accepted as the tradeoff for the latency savings. If your service requires a tighter revocation window, disable caching and accept the introspection overhead.

```python
# Cache an introspection result using the token's SHA-256 hash as the key.
import hashlib
import time

def cache_key(token: str) -> str:
    return hashlib.sha256(token.encode()).hexdigest()

def ttl_for_result(result: dict) -> int:
    if not result.get("active"):
        return 10  # Cache negative results briefly — avoids hammering introspection on replay attacks, but re-checks quickly in case of transient errors
    exp = result.get("exp", 0)
    time_to_expiry = exp - int(time.time()) - 30
    return max(0, min(60, time_to_expiry))
```

Negative results (i.e., `active: false`) are cached for 10 seconds rather than zero.
drafts.generic:
Token Introspection Endpoint

Downstream services can validate tokens by sending a POST request to /auth/introspect with the token included as a form parameter. The authorization server responds with a JSON object containing an active field, which is the most important value to check first. If active is false, the token should be rejected immediately regardless of other fields. When active is true, the response also includes sub, identifying the token subject, along with scope, exp, and client_id for additional context.

Use introspection rather than local JWT decoding when you need real-time validation, particularly for tokens that may have been revoked since issuance. Local decoding is faster and appropriate for high-volume, low-risk paths where revocation checking is not critical.
drafts.preference:
Token Introspection Endpoint

Overview

Downstream services that receive bearer tokens can validate them by calling the introspection endpoint rather than decoding the JWT locally. This section explains when to use each approach, how to make the request, and what the response fields mean.

Endpoint

POST /oauth/introspect

When to Call Introspection vs. Decode Locally

Local JWT decoding is faster and requires no network hop, so it is the right default for most validation. Use introspection instead when you need to check whether a token has been revoked, because the JWT signature will still verify correctly even after revocation — the revocation state lives server-side, not in the token itself.

Rule of thumb: decode locally for routine authorization checks on non-sensitive operations; call introspection when the action is destructive, privileged, or involves financial or personal data where acting on a stale token carries real risk.

Request Format

Send the token as a form-encoded body with your service credentials in the Authorization header.

    curl -X POST https://auth.example.com/oauth/introspect \
      -H "Authorization: Basic $(echo -n 'service-id:service-secret' | base64)" \
      -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
      -d "token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..."

The service credentials here are your downstream service's own client credentials, not the end user's token. Each service that calls introspection must be registered and credentialed separately — this is intentional so we can audit which services are inspecting which tokens.

Response Fields

A successful introspection returns JSON. The active field is the only one you must check first.

    {
      "active": true,
      "sub": "user_8f3a92c1",
      "client_id": "mobile-app-prod",
      "scope": "read:orders write:cart",
      "exp": 1718390400,
      "iat": 1718386800,
      "token_type": "Bearer",
      "service_claims": {
        "tenant_id": "acme-corp",
        "tier": "enterprise"
      }
    }

active — Boolean. If false, stop processing immediately. The token is expired, revoked, or was never valid. The remaining fields may be absent or unreliable when active is false.

sub — The internal user identifier. This is stable across sessions; use it as your foreign key if you are associating the token with a local record.

client_id — Which application originally obtained this token. Useful if your service needs to apply different logic depending on whether the caller is the mobile app, the web app, or a machine-to-machine client.

scope — Space-separated list of granted scopes. Validate that the scopes you require are present before proceeding. Do not assume that a valid token automatically carries every scope your endpoint needs.

exp and iat — Standard JWT expiry and issued-at timestamps, echoed here for convenience. These match what is in the token itself.

service_claims — A custom object we add on top of the RFC 7662 standard. It carries tenant and tier information that authorization logic frequently needs. These values are authoritative from the introspection response; do not attempt to extract them by decoding the JWT directly, as the claim structure in the raw token differs from what is surfaced here.

Error Responses

If your service credentials are invalid or missing, you will receive a 401. If the request is malformed, you will receive a 400 with an error field explaining the problem. A token that is simply inactive or revoked still returns a 200 — the active: false pattern is how RFC 7662 handles that case, and we follow it.

    {
      "active": false
    }

Do not treat a 200 with active: false as an error on the introspection call itself. It is a valid, successful response telling you the token is not usable.

Caching Introspection Results

You may cache a positive introspection result, but the cache TTL must not exceed 30 seconds. We rotate revocations into effect within that window, and a longer TTL would defeat the point of calling introspection at all. Cache the result keyed on the raw token string, not on the sub or any decoded claim.

DRAFT A (rubric-guided):
# Token Introspection Endpoint

## Before You Write Any Validation Code

> **Prerequisite:** Your service must be registered with the auth platform before making introspection calls. Registration provides you a `client_id` and `client_secret` used to authenticate the introspection request itself. Without these, every call returns `401 Unauthorized` — not a malformed token response, but a flat rejection of your service's identity. Complete registration at `auth-platform.internal/register` before proceeding.

> **Warning — clock skew:** If you're considering local JWT validation, note that the platform's token-signing nodes operate on NTP-synced clocks, but your service may not. A skew of more than 30 seconds causes false `exp` failures in local validation. Introspection sidesteps this entirely because expiry evaluation happens server-side.

---

## Deciding Whether to Call Introspection or Decode Locally

You have two paths for validating an inbound token: decode the JWT locally using the platform's published public keys, or call the introspection endpoint and let the auth server evaluate the token authoritatively.

**Local decoding** is faster — it eliminates a network hop and adds roughly 0–2ms of crypto overhead versus 15–80ms round-trip for introspection under normal load. Choose local decoding when:

- The token is being validated on a hot path (e.g., every inbound gRPC frame)
- The only signal you need is "is this token structurally valid and unexpired?"
- Revocation is not a concern for your threat model (see below)

**Introspection is required when:**

- The user or service account may have had their token revoked mid-session (e.g., account suspension, credential rotation, forced logout)
- You need claims that aren't embedded in the JWT — specifically `scope` grants added after token issuance, or dynamic entitlements managed outside the token payload
- You are a high-privilege service where acting on a revoked token has a large blast radius (e.g., billing, user data export, admin operations)

The rejected alternative here — validating only locally for all cases — was ruled out because revoked tokens remain cryptographically valid until expiry. A token with a 24-hour TTL issued to a compromised credential would pass local validation for up to 24 hours after revocation. The blast radius on privileged services made this unacceptable.

A second rejected alternative — requiring introspection for every request on every service — was ruled out on performance grounds. At p99, introspection adds 120ms under load. Applying that unconditionally to high-frequency internal services would materially degrade latency budgets. The tiered approach — introspect at privilege boundaries, decode locally elsewhere — keeps the overhead targeted.

---

## Setting Up Your Service to Call the Endpoint

Register your service at `https://auth-platform.internal/register` and retain the `client_id` and `client_secret` from the response. These credentials authenticate your service to the introspection endpoint via HTTP Basic Auth. Store them in your secrets manager, not in environment variables committed to source control.

The introspection endpoint is:

```
POST https://auth-platform.internal/oauth2/introspect
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Authorization: Basic <base64(client_id:client_secret)>

token=<token_to_validate>
```

Pass the raw token string — do not decode or strip the signature before sending.

---

## Making the Introspection Call

```python
# Send a token to the introspection endpoint and return the parsed response body.
import base64
import httpx

def introspect_token(token: str, client_id: str, client_secret: str) -> dict:
    credentials = base64.b64encode(f"{client_id}:{client_secret}".encode()).decode()
    response = httpx.post(
        "https://auth-platform.internal/oauth2/introspect",
        headers={
            "Authorization": f"Basic {credentials}",
            "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
        },
        data={"token": token},
        timeout=5.0,  # 5s chosen to exceed p99.9 server latency (measured at 3.2s) while failing fast on hung connections
    )
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response.json()
```

The 5-second timeout reflects the measured p99.9 server response time of 3.2 seconds. A lower value (e.g., 2 seconds) would produce false timeout failures during transient auth-platform load spikes; a higher value (e.g., 10 seconds) would allow a degraded auth platform to hold open connections long enough to exhaust your service's connection pool.

---

## Reading the Response Fields

A successful call returns a JSON object conforming to RFC 7662. The fields your service should act on:

**`active` (boolean, always present)**
The only field guaranteed present. `true` means the token is valid, unexpired, and not revoked as of the time of this call. `false` means any of: revoked, expired, malformed, or issued by an untrusted issuer. When `active` is `false`, no other fields are reliable — the auth platform deliberately omits them to avoid leaking information about invalid tokens.

**`sub` (string)**
The subject of the token — typically a user UUID or service account identifier. This is the canonical identifier you should use for downstream authorization checks. Do not use the `preferred_username` or `email` fields for identity decisions; those can change.

**`scope` (space-delimited string)**
The granted scopes as evaluated at the time of the call. This may differ from the `scope` claim embedded in the JWT if scope grants were modified post-issuance. For services that make authorization decisions based on scope, always use the introspection response value, not the JWT claim.

**`exp` (integer, Unix epoch)**
Expiry timestamp. Present when `active` is `true`. You don't need to evaluate this yourself — the `active` field already accounts for it — but it's useful for logging and for decisions about whether to cache the result (covered below).

**`iat` (integer, Unix epoch)**
Issuance timestamp. Useful for detecting unusually old tokens being replayed.

**`client_id` (string)**
The OAuth client that issued this token. Use this to enforce service-to-service trust boundaries: if your service should only accept tokens issued by a specific upstream, assert this field.

**`token_type` (string)**
Either `bearer` or `dpop`. If your service requires DPoP-bound tokens (i.e., you've opted into sender-constrained tokens to prevent relay attacks), reject any response where this is `bearer`.

---

## Caching Introspection Results to Reduce Latency

Calling introspection on every request defeats the performance rationale for using it selectively. Cache the result, keyed by the full token string (or its SHA-256 hash, to avoid storing raw tokens in your cache layer).

Set cache TTL to the minimum of:
- 60 seconds (hard cap regardless of token expiry)
- `exp - now - 30` seconds (expire the cache entry 30 seconds before the token expires)

The 60-second cap is not arbitrary: the auth platform propagates revocations to all signing nodes within 45 seconds under normal conditions. Caching beyond 60 seconds means your service may act on a revoked token for up to 15 seconds past the propagation window — a gap your security team has accepted as the tradeoff for the latency savings. If your service requires a tighter revocation window, disable caching and accept the introspection overhead.

```python
# Cache an introspection result using the token's SHA-256 hash as the key.
import hashlib
import time

def cache_key(token: str) -> str:
    return hashlib.sha256(token.encode()).hexdigest()

def ttl_for_result(result: dict) -> int:
    if not result.get("active"):
        return 10  # Cache negative results briefly — avoids hammering introspection on replay attacks, but re-checks quickly in case of transient errors
    exp = result.get("exp", 0)
    time_to_expiry = exp - int(time.time()) - 30
    return max(0, min(60, time_to_expiry))
```

Negative results (i.e., `active: false`) are cached for 10 seconds rather than zero.

DRAFT B (generic):
Token Introspection Endpoint

Downstream services can validate tokens by sending a POST request to /auth/introspect with the token included as a form parameter. The authorization server responds with a JSON object containing an active field, which is the most important value to check first. If active is false, the token should be rejected immediately regardless of other fields. When active is true, the response also includes sub, identifying the token subject, along with scope, exp, and client_id for additional context.

Use introspection rather than local JWT decoding when you need real-time validation, particularly for tokens that may have been revoked since issuance. Local decoding is faster and appropriate for high-volume, low-risk paths where revocation checking is not critical.

DRAFT C (original preference):
Token Introspection Endpoint

Overview

Downstream services that receive bearer tokens can validate them by calling the introspection endpoint rather than decoding the JWT locally. This section explains when to use each approach, how to make the request, and what the response fields mean.

Endpoint

POST /oauth/introspect

When to Call Introspection vs. Decode Locally

Local JWT decoding is faster and requires no network hop, so it is the right default for most validation. Use introspection instead when you need to check whether a token has been revoked, because the JWT signature will still verify correctly even after revocation — the revocation state lives server-side, not in the token itself.

Rule of thumb: decode locally for routine authorization checks on non-sensitive operations; call introspection when the action is destructive, privileged, or involves financial or personal data where acting on a stale token carries real risk.

Request Format

Send the token as a form-encoded body with your service credentials in the Authorization header.

    curl -X POST https://auth.example.com/oauth/introspect \
      -H "Authorization: Basic $(echo -n 'service-id:service-secret' | base64)" \
      -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
      -d "token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..."

The service credentials here are your downstream service's own client credentials, not the end user's token. Each service that calls introspection must be registered and credentialed separately — this is intentional so we can audit which services are inspecting which tokens.

Response Fields

A successful introspection returns JSON. The active field is the only one you must check first.

    {
      "active": true,
      "sub": "user_8f3a92c1",
      "client_id": "mobile-app-prod",
      "scope": "read:orders write:cart",
      "exp": 1718390400,
      "iat": 1718386800,
      "token_type": "Bearer",
      "service_claims": {
        "tenant_id": "acme-corp",
        "tier": "enterprise"
      }
    }

active — Boolean. If false, stop processing immediately. The token is expired, revoked, or was never valid. The remaining fields may be absent or unreliable when active is false.

sub — The internal user identifier. This is stable across sessions; use it as your foreign key if you are associating the token with a local record.

client_id — Which application originally obtained this token. Useful if your service needs to apply different logic depending on whether the caller is the mobile app, the web app, or a machine-to-machine client.

scope — Space-separated list of granted scopes. Validate that the scopes you require are present before proceeding. Do not assume that a valid token automatically carries every scope your endpoint needs.

exp and iat — Standard JWT expiry and issued-at timestamps, echoed here for convenience. These match what is in the token itself.

service_claims — A custom object we add on top of the RFC 7662 standard. It carries tenant and tier information that authorization logic frequently needs. These values are authoritative from the introspection response; do not attempt to extract them by decoding the JWT directly, as the claim structure in the raw token differs from what is surfaced here.

Error Responses

If your service credentials are invalid or missing, you will receive a 401. If the request is malformed, you will receive a 400 with an error field explaining the problem. A token that is simply inactive or revoked still returns a 200 — the active: false pattern is how RFC 7662 handles that case, and we follow it.

    {
      "active": false
    }

Do not treat a 200 with active: false as an error on the introspection call itself. It is a valid, successful response telling you the token is not usable.

Caching Introspection Results

You may cache a positive introspection result, but the cache TTL must not exceed 30 seconds. We rotate revocations into effect within that window, and a longer TTL would defeat the point of calling introspection at all. Cache the result keyed on the raw token string, not on the sub or any decoded claim.

> **Warning:** Token revocation is not instantaneous across all service instances. There is a propagation window, configurable but defaulting to 30 seconds, during which a revoked token may still be accepted by instances that have not yet refreshed their local cache.

When a user logs out or changes their password, the auth service writes the token's `jti` claim to a shared Redis revocation list with a TTL matching the token's remaining lifetime. We rejected a broadcast invalidation approach — pushing revocation events to every instance via a message queue — because it introduced tight coupling and failure modes during queue outages. The pull model accepts a bounded staleness window in exchange for resilience.

```python
# Write a revoked token's jti to Redis and check it on each authenticated request
def revoke_token(jti: str, ttl_seconds: int) -> None:
    redis_client.setex(f"revoked:{jti}", ttl_seconds, "1")

def is_revoked(jti: str) -> bool:
    return redis_client.exists(f"revoked:{jti}") == 1
```

Each instance checks `is_revoked` on every authenticated request. The revocation list is the source of truth; local in-memory caches must not be used here.</draft>
